frameworks.name: correlated variability / noise correlations
frameworks.one_line: shared fluctuations between sensory and motor regions encode task state

frameworks.name: flexible sensory-motor mapping
frameworks.one_line: arbitrary stimulus-response rules switched within a session

frameworks.name: belief-state inference
frameworks.one_line: behavioral model reconstructs participants' belief about the active rule

core_question: How does the brain implement flexible mappings between sensory input and motor action across changing task rules?
key_assumptions: participants maintain a latent belief about the currently active rule
stimulus- and action-selective activity can be isolated in visual and action-related cortex
correlated variability across regions reflects task-relevant coupling
manipulations: two arbitrary rules mapping visual orientation to a motor report
rule-switch signaling: explicit cue vs ambiguous cue requiring inference
trials with correct vs erroneous behavioral responses
key_findings: correlation patterns between stimulus- and action-selective signals reconfigured by active rule
these correlation patterns tracked participants' belief about the active rule
rule-specific correlation structure broke down around behavioral errors
effect held under both explicit and inferred rule-switch contexts
interpretations: internal beliefs about task state are instantiated in brain-wide patterns of correlated variability
flexible sensory-motor mapping is implemented through dynamic reconfiguration of cross-region coupling
